problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem A (continuous): build a cyclist power-curve model and use it to derive an optimal power-allocation (pacing) strategy for individual time trials on three courses — a self-designed loop, the 2021 UCI World Championship course in Flanders/Belgium, and the 2021 Tokyo Olympic course — for four rider archetypes (sprinter vs. time-trial specialist, male vs. female). The model must be tested for sensitivity to weather (wind speed/direction) and to rider deviation from the target power plan, extended to a 6-person team time trial (team time set by the 4th finisher), and accompanied by a 2-page non-technical race-guidance letter to a Directeur Sportif.
models_used: Three-stage power-duration model splitting the cyclist's max-power curve by exercise-intensity domain: (I) Extreme — Anaerobic Power Reserve, P(t)=P3+(Pmax-P3)e^{-kt}; (II) Severe — 2-parameter Critical Power model, P(t)=W'/t+CP; (III) Heavy — Puchowicz 'omni-domain' power-duration model with a logarithmic decay term
Parameter estimation from sampled rider data: CP=(12*P12-3*P3)/9, W'=0.24*(P3-P12), plus MAP_TTF and CP_TTF as the breakpoints between the three domains; Pmax≈P5s

Human Energy Expenditure Model: an energy-budget constraint λ_E(t)=1-∫ dτ/T(P_out(τ)), where T(P) is the inverse of the power-duration curve (max holdable time at power P); finish constraint relaxed from 1.0 to 0.95 to keep a 5% reserve

Dynamic (Newtonian) bicycle model: F_total=(F_roll+F_slope+F_accel+F_wind)/η with rolling resistance C_r*Mg, slope Mg*sinθ, inertial aM, aerodynamic ½C_W A ρ v², drivetrain efficiency η=0.95; combined with m*P_out=F_total*v to get the P_out–v conversion per road type

Piecewise nonlinear programming: minimize total time t = Σ segment_length / v(P_segment) over straights/uphills/downhills subject to the 0.95 energy-budget constraint; solved with Mathematica's Minimize on a discretized P_out(t)

Cornering kinematics model: max corner speed v_max from centripetal-force vs. friction limit Mv²/R ≤ Mgμcosα, with an optimal-racing-line geometry relating turn radius R to inner diameter r, road width d, and turn angle θ

Team time-trial drafting/formation model: drag-reduction coefficients K_f per pace-line position (drafting reduces drag to ~47-70%), with three race phases (Balance/Storage/Sprint) mapped to pyramid, long-snake, and shield formations, plus a diagonal echelon for crosswind
key_techniques: Domain decomposition of a hard physiological curve into three literature-backed regimes, each with the model the sports-science literature actually validates for that duration (APR for <3min, CP for severe, omni-domain for heavy)
Inverse-function trick: T(P)=P^{-1}(t) turns the power curve into a 'max-time-at-power' budget, making energy a clean integral constraint instead of an ad-hoc rule

Course-as-piecewise-segments abstraction: every real track is hand-digitized from Google Earth / UCI data into straights, up/down ramps, and curves (Belgium→32 segments, Tokyo→68 segments) so one programming template solves all three courses

Length-weighted slope aggregation to aid convergence: grouping many sub-1° (and 2°-4°) ramps into one 'integrated slope' using sinθ≈linear at small angles (w_k=U_k/ΣU_q), which the authors explicitly cite as fixing nonlinear-programming non-convergence

Special-case carve-out: the two genuinely hard features (Tokyo's #24-27 steep climbing hairpin and #55-56 sharp 166.9° turn) are pulled out of the LP and solved by hand with the cornering-kinematics formula
Validation against real champions as ground truth: completion times benchmarked to actual UCI/Olympic winners (within ~6-8%), and a 'moderate athletes reach near-champion times' framing

Two-axis sensitivity: weather decomposed into crosswind (shown ~<1% effect) vs. headwind (~10% on straights), and rider-deviation modeled by spline-interpolating the discrete plan and adding a bounded random disturbance term (3 Monte-Carlo-style runs) showing all riders still finish within 2.5% time lag
Insight-to-extension chain: the headwind-sensitivity finding is explicitly reused as the motivation for the team-drafting strategy, giving the paper a single causal narrative
why_it_won: From a judging perspective this paper wins because it is end-to-end coherent and grounded. It does not invent physiology — it pulls the correct, citation-backed model for each duration domain (CP, APR, omni-domain) and a textbook Newtonian force balance, then fuses them through one elegant idea (the inverse power curve as an energy budget) into a single optimization. It is genuinely tested against reality: completion times sit within 6-8% of the actual Tokyo/Belgium champions, which is the kind of external validation judges reward heavily. The sensitivity analysis is not boilerplate — it produces an actionable finding (headwind matters, crosswind doesn't) that the paper then converts into the team-strategy section, so every part feeds the next. It handles all four rider archetypes and both genders concretely with numbers, handles the messy special turns by hand instead of hiding them, and closes with a polished, on-brand 'RIDERMAN' letter that translates the math for a non-technical Directeur Sportif. Strong figures, clear assumptions with justification, and an honest weaknesses section round out a paper that reads as competent, complete, and self-aware.
section_highlights: Summary leads with a memorable hook (the Tokyo champion Anna van der Breggen holds a math PhD) and immediately presents result tables with the 6% benchmark — judges see accuracy up front. The Notations table and assumption-with-justification format (temperature/humidity range, linear energy model, constant 5% drivetrain loss, each backed by a citation) signal rigor early. The flowchart (Fig. 2) gives a one-glance map of the whole paper. The modeling sections build strictly bottom-up: power curve → energy budget → force balance → P_out-v conversion → piecewise NLP, so the optimization feels inevitable rather than arbitrary. Real-course sections show actual Google Earth digitization and segment counts, which reads as real work. The sensitivity section is visual and conclusion-driven, and crucially seeds the team section. The closing Directeur Sportif letter is genuinely audience-aware: branded 'RIDERMAN', conversational, with fill-in tables and percentage rules of thumb (U6 uphill +8-10%, D6 downhill -30%) instead of equations.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Power-curve parameters come from 'average rider' data, not elite riders, so absolute times trail champions and the authors must hand-wave the gap (e.g. female model 34.63min vs. champion 30.22min — a ~14% miss that is glossed as 'reasonable')

Internal inconsistency / sloppy numbers: the Task-2 self-designed and Japan result tables have columns that look swapped or mismatched (e.g. Tokyo male sprinter 62.68min but male TT specialist 40.97min — physically odd), and several inline values are garbled in the text (Pmax^{#27}, the #55-56 units in Table 9). Judges tolerate it here but it is a real quality risk.

The cornering analysis is largely decoupled from the energy optimization — turns are first declared negligible (0.185km vs 36.5km) and approximated as step changes in power, so the LP and the corner model never truly co-optimize
Discretizing P_out(t) and the length-weighted slope aggregation are convergence hacks that trade accuracy for solvability; the paper admits numerical/discrete methods cap precision

The team time-trial section is more qualitative than the rest: drag coefficients and the Balance/Storage/Sprint 48/27/25 split lean on a single cited aerodynamics paper (Blocken 2018) rather than the team's own model, and the '4th rider' team-time definition is never explicitly optimized
Weather model ignores temperature/humidity and rain by assumption rather than analysis, and treats air density as a fixed constant
No adversary/tactics modeling (acknowledged) — purely an unopposed time-trial, which is fine for the prompt but limits generality claims
reusable_patterns: Domain-decompose a hard nonlinear relationship into regimes, fit each regime with the model the domain literature already validates, then stitch with continuity conditions — far more defensible than one global curve fit
Convert a 'budget' resource (energy, fuel, money) into an integral constraint via an inverse function, turning a physiological/empirical curve into clean optimization machinery
Build a self-designed toy instance first to develop the per-feature strategy, then apply the same template to the real (messy) instances — gives a clean methods section and reusable logic
Hand-digitize real geography into a piecewise segment list (straights/ramps/curves) so one optimization template generalizes across all required scenarios
Aggregate many near-identical small components using a linear-approximation weighting to keep a nonlinear solver convergent, and disclose it as a deliberate simplification
Pull genuinely hard sub-cases out of the global optimizer and solve them analytically/by hand, then re-insert — keeps the main model tractable without ignoring difficulty
Benchmark model output against real-world ground truth (actual champions) and report the percentage gap as the headline accuracy claim
Make sensitivity analysis productive: let one sensitivity finding (headwind) directly motivate a later required extension (team drafting), giving the paper a single causal spine
Model rider/operator deviation by interpolating the ideal plan and adding a bounded random disturbance, then run a few trials to show robustness — a cheap, convincing stochastic stress test
Brand the deliverable (here 'RIDERMAN') and write the non-technical letter with fill-in tables and percentage rules of thumb, including a real-world expert quote as a credibility hook
